classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

preferredAgent.sig: estradiol transdermal patch 0.05 mg/day, apply 1 patch to skin twice weekly
preferredAgent.plainSig: Apply one patch to your skin twice a week, rotating the spot each time

records.verificationNote: The NYRx PDP PDL (the payer's actual, current, correctly-dated formulary PA document, revised 2026-06-04, fetched and read in full: 85 pages, all 15 top-level drug-class sections) does not contain a menopausal/systemic hormone-replacement-therapy or estrogen-therapy section anywhere in its Preferred Drug Program (pages 4-59), Clinical Drug Review Program (page 60), Frequency/Quantity/Duration or Step Therapy program (pages 61-74), or Brand Less Than Generic program (pages 76-77). Searches of the extracted text for 'estradiol', 'menopaus', 'progesterone', and 'Premarin' confirm the only estrogen-related entry in the whole document is a narrow 'Hormone Replacement Therapy for Treatment of Gender Dysphoria' DUR-program listing (page 66) -- a different clinical indication, not menopausal HT -- and that Premarin appears once, only in the Brand-Less-Than-Generic list (page 76), never in a preferred/non-preferred estrogen-class table. The document's own overview (page 1) states 'Not all drugs covered by NYRx are subject to programs included in this document,' meaning transdermal estradiol is very likely covered under standard NYRx pharmacy benefits without a PA-based preferred-agent restriction -- but that is an inference, not something read verbatim as a stated 'preferred agent' for this class, so per instructions this is marked example rather than fabricating a preferred-agent finding attributed to a source that does not state one. A WebSearch for 'NYRx New York Medicaid preferred drug list estrogen hormone replacement therapy prior authorization' returned no separate payer-specific document naming a preferred transdermal estrogen agent either.

preferredAgent.sig: estradiol 1 mg tablet, take 1 tablet by mouth once daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one tablet by mouth once a day

records.verificationNote: The NYRx PDP PDL (the payer's actual, current, correctly-dated formulary PA document, revised 2026-06-04, fetched and read in full across all 15 top-level drug-class sections) contains no Preferred Drug List section for oral estrogen / menopausal hormone therapy. The only estrogen-related listing anywhere in the document is the 'Hormone Replacement Therapy for Treatment of Gender Dysphoria' DUR entry (page 66), a distinct clinical indication, and a single mention of Premarin in the Brand-Less-Than-Generic table (page 76) with no accompanying preferred/non-preferred oral-estrogen class table. Since the document explicitly notes 'Not all drugs covered by NYRx are subject to programs included in this document,' oral estradiol/conjugated estrogens are likely covered under ordinary NYRx pharmacy benefits without a PA-restricted preferred designation, but no source read for this task states an actual preferred oral-estrogen agent, tier, or restriction, so this is marked example rather than presenting an inferred agent as verified fact. A supporting WebSearch for the payer's hormone-therapy PA policy surfaced no separate document naming a preferred oral estrogen.

preferredAgent.sig: progesterone (micronized) 100 mg capsule, take 1 capsule by mouth at bedtime for 12 days per cycle (or per prescriber protocol)
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one capsule by mouth at bedtime, on the schedule your prescriber gave you, to protect the lining of your uterus while you're on estrogen therapy

records.verificationNote: The NYRx PDP PDL (the payer's actual, current, correctly-dated formulary PA document, revised 2026-06-04, read in full) has no progestogen or progesterone class table anywhere -- a text search for 'progesterone' across the whole 85-page extracted document returned zero matches. No preferred/non-preferred designation for medroxyprogesterone, micronized progesterone, or norethindrone acetate is stated in this source. Given the document's own disclosure that not all covered drugs are subject to its programs, progestogens are likely covered under standard NYRx benefits without a PA-restricted preferred agent, but that is an inference, not a verbatim finding, so this class is marked example rather than asserting a specific preferred agent as sourced fact.

preferredAgent.sig: estradiol vaginal cream 0.01%, insert 2-4 g intravaginally once daily for 1-2 weeks, then 1-3 g 1-3 times weekly for maintenance
preferredAgent.plainSig: Insert into the vagina daily for the first couple of weeks, then cut back to a few times a week, as directed

records.verificationNote: The NYRx PDP PDL (the payer's actual, current, correctly-dated formulary PA document, revised 2026-06-04, read in full) contains no vaginal/local estrogen class table. A search of the extracted text for 'Vagifem' (a common branded vaginal estradiol tablet) returned zero matches, and no other vaginal-estrogen product (cream, ring, or insert) appears anywhere in the document's Preferred Drug Program, CDRP, DUR, or BLTG sections. As with the other menopausal-hormone classes, the document's disclosure that 'not all drugs covered by NYRx are subject to programs included in this document' suggests vaginal estrogen is covered under ordinary benefits without a PA-restricted preferred designation, but this is an inference rather than a sourced fact, so the class is marked example.

preferredAgent.sig: estradiol/norethindrone acetate 1 mg/0.5 mg tablet, take 1 tablet by mouth once daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one combination tablet by mouth once a day

records.verificationNote: The NYRx PDP PDL (the payer's actual, current, correctly-dated formulary PA document, revised 2026-06-04, read in full) contains no oral estrogen-progestin combination class table. No branded or generic combined HT product (e.g., Activella/estradiol-norethindrone, Prempro/conjugated estrogens-medroxyprogesterone) appears in the Preferred Drug Program, CDRP, DUR, or BLTG sections of the extracted text. Consistent with the other hormone-therapy classes in this payer's document, this is very likely covered under standard NYRx benefits without a PA-restricted preferred designation, but since no source read for this task states a preferred combination agent, this class is marked example rather than presenting an inferred pick as verified.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'ESTROGENS' subsection spans document pages 71-72: CLIMARA Tier 3 QL, DIVIGEL (multiple strengths/packet sizes) Tier 3 QL, dotti Tier 1, ELESTRIN Tier 3, estradiol oral Tier 1 (separate oral entry, not counted here), estradiol transdermal gel in metered-dose pump Tier 3, estradiol transdermal gel in packet Tier 3, estradiol transdermal patch semiweekly Tier 1 (no QL flag), estradiol transdermal patch weekly Tier 1 QL, estradiol valerate Tier 1 (injectable/oral form, not transdermal), ESTROGEL Tier 3, EVAMIST Tier 2, lyllana Tier 1, MENOSTAR Tier 3 QL, MINIVELLE Tier 3, PREMARIN ORAL Tier 2 (oral, not transdermal), VIVELLE-DOT Tier 3. No dedicated header separates 'transdermal' from 'oral' within this ESTROGENS section -- entries were classified as transdermal (patch/gel/spray) vs oral based on the product description text. Generic estradiol transdermal patch is the representative preferred agent (Tier 1), consistent with clinical guidance that generic transdermal estradiol is the standard first-line choice given its lower VTE risk vs oral. No PA or step-therapy flag found on any transdermal estrogen product in this table. Not verified: exact patch/gel strength lineup per SKU, and whether Excellus requires a documented menopause/GSM diagnosis before coverage (not disclosed in this member-facing guide).

preferredAgent.plainSig: One pill, once a day.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). Within the 'ESTROGENS' subsection on document pages 71-72: estradiol oral is listed at Tier 1 with no restriction flags; estradiol valerate is listed at Tier 1 (this ester form is typically used orally or as an injectable, included here as an oral-class alternative); PREMARIN ORAL (conjugated estrogens) is listed at Tier 2 with no restriction flags. No generic conjugated-estrogens product appears in this table, so Premarin Oral is the only conjugated-estrogen option and sits above the generic estradiol tablet in tier/cost. No PA or step-therapy flag on any oral estrogen product. Not verified: specific oral estradiol tablet strength lineup (0.5/1/2 mg) since the table lists a single unstrengthed 'estradiol oral' line item rather than itemizing each strength.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'PROGESTINS' subsection on document page 74 lists: GALLIFREY Tier 1, medroxyprogesterone oral Tier 1, norethindrone acetate Tier 1, progesterone Tier 1 MS;S, progesterone micronized oral Tier 1 (no restriction flags). Micronized oral progesterone is selected as the representative preferred agent per the class description's stated clinical preference ('micronized progesterone preferred') for endometrial protection alongside systemic estrogen, and it is the only progestogen in this table with zero restriction flags. The 'progesterone' line item (MS;S -- specialty pharmacy required) is distinct from 'progesterone micronized oral' and likely represents an injectable/compounded form requiring specialty handling; it is listed as an alternative, not folded into the preferred pick, since its restriction profile differs. No PA or step-therapy flag on the micronized oral capsule itself. Not verified: exact capsule strength options (100 mg vs 200 mg) since the table lists a single unstrengthed line item.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'VAGINAL ESTROGENS' subsection on document page 128 lists exactly three items: estradiol vaginal Tier 1, PREMARIN VAGINAL Tier 2, yuvafem Tier 1 -- no restriction flags (PA/QL/ST/MS/S/PV) on any of the three. Generic estradiol vaginal is used as the representative preferred agent given its Tier 1 placement and consistency with the class description (low-dose vaginal estradiol cream/tablet/insert for GSM with minimal systemic absorption). This section does not distinguish cream vs tablet vs ring/insert dosage forms by name, so the specific form (cream, tablet, or insert) for 'estradiol vaginal' is inferred as likely cream and/or insert based on common generic vaginal-estradiol products on the market, not confirmed verbatim in the document text. No vaginal estradiol ring (e.g., Estring) appears anywhere in this document. Not verified: exact strength/dosage-form breakdown for 'estradiol vaginal' and 'yuvafem'.

The 'ESTROGEN-PROGESTIN' subsection on document page 71 lists: ABIGALE Tier 1, ABIGALE LO Tier 1, CLIMARA PRO Tier 2, estradiol-norethindrone acet Tier 1, fyavolv Tier 1, jinteli Tier 1, mimvey Tier 1, norethindrone ac-eth estradiol oral tablet (two strength combos) Tier 1, PREMPHASE Tier 2, PREMPRO Tier 2 -- no restriction flags (PA/QL/ST/MS/S/PV) on any item. Generic estradiol-norethindrone acetate oral tablet is used as the representative preferred agent, matching the class description's note that 'generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass' -- this is a direct, near-verbatim match between the class definition and the document's own product naming. Climara Pro is the only transdermal combination product in this section (all others are oral); it sits at Tier 2. No PA or step-therapy flag on any combo product in this table. 'estradiol transdermal patch twice weekly' and 'estradiol transdermal patch weekly' are both listed at Tier 1 with QL, no PA/ST flag. Two brand-name twice-weekly patches, DOTTI and LYLLANA, are listed at the same Tier 1 with QL - unusual for brands to share the generic's tier, but read verbatim from the document; both are alternatives at equal cost-share rather than the single preferred pick. No transdermal gel or spray estradiol product (e.g. Divigel, Elestrin, Evamist) appears anywhere in the estrogens section of this document - patches are the only transdermal delivery format on this formulary as written.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off page 71 of the fetched PDF, under the *PROGESTINS* subsection, page footer 'Effective 07012026'. 'progesterone oral capsule' (the micronized/Prometrium-equivalent generic) is listed at Tier 1 with no Notes column entry (no QL, PA, or ST) - the cleanest possible coverage. medroxyprogesterone acetate oral tablet, norethindrone acetate oral tablet, and the brand GALLIFREY oral tablet all sit at the identical Tier 1 with no restrictions, making them true same-tier alternatives rather than higher-cost options. This document lists no injectable progestin-only product in this specific subsection (the injectable medroxyprogesterone acetate for contraception appears separately on page 44 under PROGESTIN CONTRACEPTIVES, a distinct indication from endometrial protection during systemic estrogen therapy).

records.verificationNote: Read directly off page 79 of the fetched PDF, under the *VAGINAL ESTROGENS* subsection, page footer 'Effective 07012026'. 'estradiol vaginal cream' and 'estradiol vaginal tablet' are both listed at Tier 2 with QL - this is the lowest tier available in this entire subsection (no vaginal estrogen product reaches Tier 1 in this document). Treating estradiol vaginal tablet as the preferred pick since it is the most commonly prescribed low-dose insert form and ties for lowest tier with the cream; YUVAFEM (brand-name version of the same tablet) shares the identical Tier 2/QL profile. ESTRING, IMVEXXY (both starter and maintenance packs), and PREMARIN vaginal cream all sit at Tier 3 QL - covered but highest cost-share, not PA/step barriers. No PA or ST flag appears on any vaginal estrogen product in this document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off page 54 of the fetched PDF, under the *ESTROGEN & PROGESTIN* subsection (which precedes the standalone ESTROGENS heading on the same page), page footer 'Effective 07012026'. 'estradiol-norethindrone acet oral tablet' is listed at Tier 1 with no Notes column entry - the standard generic first-pass combo per the class definition. FYAVOLV, JINTELI, MIMVEY (all brand names for the same or similar estradiol/norethindrone formulation) and 'norethindrone-eth estradiol oral tablet' (an alternate generic labeling) are all also Tier 1 with no restrictions, effectively co-preferred. BIJUVA (bioidentical estradiol-progesterone), PREMPHASE, and PREMPRO sit at Tier 3 (BIJUVA additionally QL) - highest cost-share but still covered, not PA/step barriers. No PA or ST flag appears on any product in this subsection.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs), PDF pages 73-74 (internal document pages 63-64), section 'HORMONAL AGENTS, STIMULANT/ REPLACEMENT/ MODIFYING (SEX HORMONES/ MODIFIERS)' > 'Estrogens'. Confirmed verbatim: 'estradiol transdermal patch weekly' Tier 2, QL (4 EA per 28 days); 'estradiol transdermal patch semiweekly' Tier 2, QL (8 EA per 28 days); 'estradiol transdermal gel in metered-dose pump' Tier 3, no restriction column entry; 'estradiol transdermal gel in packet' Tier 3, no restriction; 'DOTTI' Tier 2, QL (8 EA per 28 days); 'EVAMIST' Tier 4; 'ELESTRIN' Tier 4; 'CLIMARA PRO' Tier 4, QL (4 EA per 28 days). Document effective date 7/01/2026 confirmed on cover and page 9. No brand-name patches (Climara, Vivelle-Dot, Minivelle, Alora) appear by name — the plan lists only the generic 'estradiol transdermal patch weekly/semiweekly' line items, meaning the generic patch is the sole formulary-listed monotherapy patch and is Tier 2 with no restrictions beyond quantity limit.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs), PDF page 79 (internal document page 69), section 'HORMONAL AGENTS, STIMULANT/ REPLACEMENT/ MODIFYING (SEX HORMONES/ MODIFIERS)' > 'Progestins'. Confirmed verbatim: 'progesterone micronized oral' Tier 2, no restriction listed; 'medroxyprogesterone oral' Tier 1, no restriction; 'medroxyprogesterone intramuscular' Tier 2, no restriction; 'megestrol oral tablet' Tier 2, no restriction; 'megestrol oral suspension' Tier 4. Document effective date 7/01/2026 confirmed on cover and page 9. Micronized progesterone is chosen as the representative preferred pick per clinical guideline preference (bioidentical, favorable safety profile) even though medroxyprogesterone sits one tier lower (Tier 1 vs Tier 2) — both are clean-covered with zero restrictions, so the cost/tier tradeoff is noted but the clinical preferred-agent pick follows guideline-first practice, not cheapest-tier.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs), PDF pages 74-77 (internal document pages 64-66), section 'HORMONAL AGENTS, STIMULANT/ REPLACEMENT/ MODIFYING (SEX HORMONES/ MODIFIERS)' > 'Estrogens' and 'Hormonal Agents, Stimulant/ Replacement/ Modifying (Sex Hormones/ Modifiers)'. Document effective date 7/01/2026 confirmed on cover and page 9. Note the document also lists a very large number of combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol + progestin) products in this same section (Apri, Sprintec, Junel, etc.) — these are contraceptive-dose combos for a different indication than menopausal hormone therapy and were excluded from this class's alternatives/paRequired lists as clinically non-equivalent, not omitted by oversight.
